Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a proper study routine most of the time. I'd usually study when I felt like it or when something was due soon. Sometimes I'd listen to lecture recordings while doing other things around the house, which worked better for me than sitting and reading. My study hours were scattered throughout the week without any real plan.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was happening. If there was a deadline coming up, I'd suddenly be more focused, but otherwise I'd drift for days without touching anything course-related. I never really established a consistent weekly schedule because other things always seemed to come up or I just wasn't feeling it.
Q3: Usually it was because I remembered an assignment was due or I got a notification about something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, but I wouldn't stay long. It wasn't really a habit for me, more like a reaction to realizing I needed to do something before it was too late.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine the way it probably should have. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I was logging in more regularly, but it didn't stick. I think because I don't have a lot of resources at home, using the LMS felt like an extra effort rather than something seamless.
Q5: The audio lectures and video content were definitely the most useful for me since I learn better by listening. I could play them while doing other things, which fit my life better. The discussion posts were occasionally helpful when other students explained things in simpler terms, but I didn't engage with those much myself.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials and long PDF documents because I find it hard to focus on reading for extended periods. The optional quizzes and practice exercises I usually skipped unless I was really worried about an exam. It felt like there was too much content and I didn't have the energy to go through everything.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what would be on an exam or assignment. If I could see the immediate payoff, I'd do it. Things that felt like busywork or extras that wouldn't affect my grade usually got pushed aside because I had limited time and energy to spare.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely different to me. Required stuff had this weight to it, like I knew I had to do it eventually even if I procrastinated. Optional things just fell off my radar entirely because there was no real consequence for skipping them. I rarely had the motivation to do extra work.
Q9: Definitely, I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone when I wasn't at my computer. I also borrowed notes from classmates sometimes and studied from those instead of going through the LMS. With limited internet access at times, offline studying was just more practical for me.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was going over downloaded materials or listening to recordings offline where the LMS wouldn't show anything. I'd also sometimes study with a friend who had better notes, and we'd talk through concepts together. None of that would show up in any system, but I was still learning.
Q11: Yes, all the time actually. I'd download a video or audio file once and then replay it multiple times offline over the following days. It saved data and was more convenient for me. The LMS would only show one access, but I might have reviewed that material three or four times on my own.
Q12: There were definitely weeks where everything felt harder. Sometimes I just felt disconnected from the whole university experience, like I was going through the motions without really caring. When I had other life stuff going on or felt overwhelmed, the course became the first thing I let slide.
Q13: Competing responsibilities definitely affected my engagement. With low resources, I sometimes had to prioritize other things over studying. Fatigue was also a big factor because I'd be too tired to properly engage even when I did log in. Confusion with course material sometimes made me just close the LMS instead of pushing through.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times I'd be scrolling through materials but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd have a video playing but I wasn't really absorbing anything. It was like I was going through the motions to feel like I was doing something, but I wasn't actually learning in those moments.
Q15: I postponed assignments a lot, even ones I knew were important. I think part of it was just not feeling connected to the work, and part was that starting felt overwhelming. The mindfulness stuff they introduced actually helped a bit with the anxiety around starting, but I still struggled with procrastination throughout the course.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main thing that got me to actually do work. Without a deadline looming, I'd drift and not engage much at all. When something was due in a day or two, suddenly I'd be on the LMS downloading everything and trying to catch up. It wasn't healthy but it's how I operated.
Q17: Feedback did affect me when I actually got it. If I got decent marks, I'd feel a bit more motivated for the next thing. But honestly, I didn't always check feedback right away, and sometimes by the time I saw it, I'd already moved on mentally. The delay in my response to things was a pattern.
Q18: The course structure felt a bit overwhelming with all the different components. I sometimes got lost in what was required versus optional, and that confusion made me disengage. If things had been simpler or more clearly laid out, I might have participated more consistently instead of just catching up before deadlines.
Q19: The video content being available to download made engagement easier for me since I could study offline. But the amount of reading materials and the way assignments were spread out made it harder. I also found the discussion requirements a bit awkward since I'm not naturally inclined to post my thoughts publicly.
Q20: I think some students might be genuinely trying to understand the material by exploring everything, but then struggle when it comes to actually producing work. Or maybe they're anxious about submitting things that aren't perfect. There's also the possibility they're just clicking around without really engaging, which I've done myself.
Q21: That actually reflects my experience somewhat. I didn't log in that much or post in discussions, but I was studying offline with downloaded materials and still managed to pass. The LMS activity doesn't capture everything, and some people just learn differently or have circumstances that keep them away from the platform.
Q22: A course log can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but it can't show if you actually understood anything or if you were really paying attention. It misses all the offline studying, the conversations with classmates, and honestly, it can't tell the difference between someone engaged and someone just scrolling mindlessly.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just doing the minimum to pass. For me, I was rarely truly engaged this semester, more just surviving. Real engagement would mean I'm curious, asking questions, and thinking about the material even when I don't have to.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was dealing with outside of class, or how my low resources affected my ability to stay connected. It wouldn't capture the times I was genuinely interested but couldn't access materials, or the studying I did offline. It also can't show that even though I drifted, I still cared enough to pass.
